channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK mortgage and housing finance news, sourced and rewritten daily.

item.title: Have you lost a UK mortgage deal or seen your mortage rate increase? We would like to speak to you
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description: By Nadya Putri Maharani The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has had far-reaching consequences, extending beyond geopolitical boundaries and impacting global markets. In the UK, one notable effect...
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has had far-reaching consequences, extending beyond geopolitical boundaries and impacting global markets. In the UK, one notable effect has been the ripple through the mortgage sector, with lenders responding swiftly to the uncertainty. Within just 48 hours of the conflict escalating, hundreds of mortgage products were withdrawn, replaced by deals featuring higher interest rates. This shift has left many borrowers facing increased costs and disrupted plans.

Impact on the UK Mortgage Market

The withdrawal of mortgage products and the introduction of higher rates have coincided with a decline in average house prices. Halifax reported a 0.5% drop in March, a trend partly attributed to reduced demand as higher borrowing costs deterred potential buyers. For homeowners and prospective buyers, this has created a challenging landscape. Those who had secured favourable rates may find their deals no longer available, while others may face unexpected increases in their monthly repayments.

The broader economic climate, including rising oil prices and inflation concerns, has further compounded the situation. Lenders, wary of potential instability, have adjusted their offerings to mitigate risk. This has left many borrowers navigating a market that feels increasingly uncertain.

Navigating Higher Mortgage Rates

For those affected by the changes, there are steps that could help mitigate the impact. Borrowers might consider reviewing their current mortgage terms to explore whether switching to a different product or lender could offer a more favourable rate. Additionally, speaking with a mortgage broker could provide tailored advice based on individual circumstances.

Homeowners who have seen their fixed-rate deals expire may wish to act quickly to secure a new deal, as delaying could result in being moved to a lender’s standard variable rate, which is often significantly higher. Meanwhile, prospective buyers might consider reassessing their budgets and timelines to ensure they remain comfortable with their financial commitments.

The Broader Implications

The current turbulence in the mortgage market highlights the interconnected nature of global events and their local impacts. For many, the situation serves as a reminder of the importance of staying informed and proactive when it comes to financial planning. Whilst the immediate future may feel uncertain, taking steps to understand available options could help borrowers navigate this challenging period.

As the market continues to evolve, staying attuned to changes and seeking professional guidance could prove invaluable. Whether adjusting plans or exploring new opportunities, borrowers may find that taking a measured approach helps them manage the current climate more effectively.

item.title: UK house prices fall in March amid uncertain impact of Middle East conflict
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description: By Rizky Aditya Pratama UK house prices experienced a slight dip in March, with Halifax reporting a 0.5% monthly decline that pushed the average property value back below £300,000. The lender's data...
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK house prices experienced a slight dip in March, with Halifax reporting a 0.5% monthly decline that pushed the average property value back below £300,000. The lender's data reveals annual growth slowed to 0.8%, suggesting cooling momentum as the spring selling season begins. Market analysts point to global uncertainties, including Middle East tensions, as contributing factors to this subdued performance in the housing market.

Global tensions and mortgage market impacts

The conflict in the Middle East appears to be influencing UK housing market sentiment, with Halifax noting its dampening effect on early-year momentum. Rising energy price concerns have fed into inflation expectations, subsequently pushing mortgage rates higher. Fixed-rate deals have been particularly affected, with the average two-year fixed residential mortgage rate reaching 5.84% by March's end – the highest level since July 2024.

Mortgage product availability has also tightened recently, with lenders withdrawing hundreds of deals from the market. However, some relief emerged as financial markets scaled back expectations for Bank of England rate rises following diplomatic developments in the Middle East. Current pricing suggests just one more quarter-point increase may be expected this year.

What this means for borrowers

For potential buyers, particularly first-time purchasers, mortgage rates remain a crucial consideration. Halifax's head of mortgages noted that many are already balancing deposit saving challenges with borrowing costs. While current rate increases have been more gradual than the dramatic spikes following the 2022 mini-budget, they still influence affordability calculations.

Existing homeowners on fixed-rate deals remain insulated from immediate impacts, though those approaching renewal may wish to monitor rate trends closely. The market's future direction will likely depend on how long current economic pressures persist and their broader effects on employment and consumer confidence.

As the spring market develops, prospective buyers and sellers might consider seeking independent mortgage advice to navigate these changing conditions. With various factors influencing both property prices and borrowing costs, staying informed could help households make well-considered property decisions.

item.title: UK interest rate predictions fall as US and Iran agree two-week ceasefire
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description: By Sri Wahyuni Astuti Mortgage borrowers may see some relief as financial markets scale back predictions for UK interest rate rises following geopolitical developments in the Middle East. The recent...
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Mortgage borrowers may see some relief as financial markets scale back predictions for UK interest rate rises following geopolitical developments in the Middle East. The recent US-Iran ceasefire agreement has prompted City traders to revise their forecasts, with money markets now anticipating just one potential Bank of England rate increase by December, rather than the two previously expected.

How global events affect UK mortgage rates

The shift in rate expectations comes as oil prices fell sharply following news of the temporary truce, with Brent crude dropping 13.3% to $94.71 per barrel. This development has eased concerns about prolonged supply disruptions that could have fuelled inflation. Financial analysts note that when oil prices stabilise, it often reduces pressure on central banks to raise interest rates aggressively.

Market expectations for UK base rate movements have fluctuated significantly this year. In early 2024, some economists predicted potential rate cuts, while March saw forecasts of up to three increases. The current projection of a single 0.25 percentage point rise would take the Bank Rate to 4%, still below the 5.25% peak seen in 2023.

What this means for homeowners

While swap rates – which influence fixed mortgage pricing – have begun to ease slightly, industry experts caution that borrowers shouldn't expect an immediate or dramatic fall in mortgage rates. The average two-year fixed deal has climbed from 4.83% at the beginning of March to 5.90%, reaching its highest level since July 2024.

Chris Beauchamp, chief market analyst at IG, commented: "The ceasefire news brings some relief for UK consumers, potentially easing petrol prices and reducing expectations for rate hikes. However, the prospect of sustained rate cuts seen earlier this year appears unlikely to return soon."

Mortgage seekers might consider that while the immediate upward pressure on rates may have eased, volatility in global markets means lenders could remain cautious about pricing. Those approaching the end of fixed-term deals may wish to explore their options carefully, as the full impact of these developments on mortgage products may take time to materialise.

As the situation remains fluid, borrowers would be wise to stay informed about market movements and consult independent mortgage advisers to understand how these changes might affect their individual circumstances.

item.title: Iran war may increase mortgage payments for extra 1.3m households, says Bank of England
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description: By Bambang Setiawan Escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East could lead to higher mortgage costs for an additional 1.3 million UK households, according to recent Bank of England analysis....
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East could lead to higher mortgage costs for an additional 1.3 million UK households, according to recent Bank of England analysis. The central bank warns that ongoing conflict has created significant economic instability, with lenders withdrawing hundreds of mortgage products and increasing rates on remaining deals.

Mortgage market turbulence

Financial markets have reacted sharply to Middle Eastern hostilities, with approximately 1,500 mortgage products being withdrawn from the market in recent weeks. Data shows the average two-year fixed rate has climbed from 4.83% to 5.84% since March, adding considerable pressure to household budgets. Experts note these increases have occurred rapidly, with borrowers facing steeper costs almost immediately after the conflict began.

The Bank of England's Financial Policy Committee estimates that 5.2 million borrowers – representing 58% of UK mortgage holders – could see their payments rise by 2028. This marks a significant increase from the 3.9 million households previously expected to face higher costs before the conflict erupted.

Broader economic implications

Analysts suggest prolonged instability could create multiple financial challenges, including:

  • Increased volatility in global markets
  • Pressure on government debt markets
  • Potential instability in private credit markets
  • Disruption to global supply chains

The Bank notes that while the UK financial system remains resilient, the conflict has made the global economic environment markedly less predictable. This comes at a time when existing risks – including inflated asset valuations and unregulated lending practices – were already causing concern among policymakers.

For homeowners approaching the end of fixed-rate deals or considering remortgaging, staying informed about market developments could prove valuable. Mortgage brokers may be able to help borrowers navigate the changing landscape and explore available options.

item.title: How to survive our doomed times? Both the experts and I have the same advice | Emma Brockes
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description: By Nadya Putri Maharani In times of economic uncertainty, homeowners and potential buyers may feel overwhelmed by the constant flux of market conditions. With headlines warning of rising borrowing...
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

In times of economic uncertainty, homeowners and potential buyers may feel overwhelmed by the constant flux of market conditions. With headlines warning of rising borrowing costs and potential interest rate hikes, it’s natural to question how best to navigate the mortgage landscape. Whilst experts often advise against making hasty decisions during periods of volatility, understanding the current climate and planning accordingly could help borrowers feel more secure.

The Impact of Rising Interest Rates

Recent forecasts have sparked concerns about potential increases in interest rates, with some analysts predicting multiple quarter-point rises by 2026. This marks a significant shift from earlier expectations of rate reductions. For homeowners on variable-rate mortgages or those considering remortgaging, this could mean higher monthly repayments in the future. Borrowers might consider reviewing their current mortgage terms to assess whether locking into a fixed-rate deal could provide stability amidst uncertainty.

Similarly, first-time buyers or those looking to purchase a property may wish to factor in potential rate increases when budgeting. Whilst it’s impossible to predict exact figures, seeking advice from a mortgage broker could help individuals understand their options and prepare for various scenarios.

Navigating Market Volatility

Economic instability often leads to heightened anxiety, particularly when it comes to long-term financial commitments like mortgages. However, experts frequently recommend avoiding knee-jerk reactions during turbulent times. Instead, maintaining a steady approach and focusing on long-term goals could prove more beneficial.

For those with investments linked to property, such as buy-to-let portfolios, it may be worth reassessing their strategy to ensure it aligns with current market conditions. Diversifying investments or seeking professional financial advice could help mitigate risks associated with market fluctuations.

Whilst it’s easy to feel daunted by economic uncertainties, taking proactive steps and staying informed can empower homeowners and buyers to make confident decisions. By keeping a close eye on interest rate trends and seeking expert guidance, individuals can navigate the mortgage market with greater clarity and peace of mind.

item.title: Why Bank kept interest rates on hold despite message for UK to brace itself for Trumpflation
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Bank of England has opted to keep interest rates on hold, despite warnings that households should prepare for potential economic turbulence linked to global instability. This decision comes amidst growing concerns over rising inflation, driven in part by geopolitical tensions and soaring energy prices, often referred to as "Trumpflation".

The Impact of Global Instability on Inflation

The Bank’s latest monetary policy report highlights the potential economic fallout from global conflicts, particularly those involving the US and Iran. These tensions have already driven oil and gas prices higher, with projections suggesting that UK households could face significant financial pressures. Mortgage repayments are expected to rise by an average of £80 per month, while food price inflation could reach 4.6% by autumn. Utility bills are also set to increase in July and remain elevated throughout the winter.

Inflation is now forecast to peak above 3.5% by the end of the year, a sharp increase from pre-conflict estimates. In a worst-case scenario, where oil prices climb to $130 a barrel and remain high for an extended period, inflation could exceed 6%. Such conditions might necessitate interest rate hikes of more than 1.5 percentage points, potentially pushing the base rate to at least 5.25%.

Why Rates Were Kept on Hold

Despite these inflationary pressures, the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) chose to maintain the current interest rate. Only one member, Huw Pill, the Bank’s chief economist, dissented, advocating for a rate increase. The decision reflects concerns about the UK’s fragile economic recovery, which could be further weakened by aggressive monetary tightening.

The MPC’s primary focus is on mitigating "second-round effects," where businesses pass on higher costs to consumers through price increases, and workers demand higher wages to offset rising living expenses. These dynamics could embed inflation more deeply into the economy. However, with GDP growth expected to be just 0.8% this year and unemployment projected to peak at 5.5% in 2024, the ability of workers to secure pay rises remains limited.

What This Means for Households

For UK households, the Bank’s decision offers short-term relief but underscores the challenges ahead. Rising energy costs, higher mortgage payments, and increased food prices are likely to squeeze household budgets. Meanwhile, cautious consumer spending could further dampen economic growth, creating a complex balancing act for policymakers.

Borrowers might consider reviewing their mortgage arrangements to prepare for potential rate increases in the future. Fixed-rate deals could provide stability amidst uncertainty, while homeowners may wish to explore options for reducing monthly outgoings where possible.

As the Bank of England navigates these turbulent economic waters, its cautious approach highlights the delicate interplay between inflation control and economic recovery. With global uncertainty showing no signs of abating, UK households and businesses must remain vigilant in managing their finances.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Bank of England has opted to keep interest rates on hold, despite warnings that households should prepare for potential economic turbulence linked to global instability. This decision comes amidst growing concerns over rising inflation, driven in part by geopolitical tensions and soaring energy prices, often referred to as "Trumpflation".

The Impact of Global Instability on Inflation

The Bank’s latest monetary policy report highlights the potential economic fallout from global conflicts, particularly those involving the US and Iran. These tensions have already driven oil and gas prices higher, with projections suggesting that UK households could face significant financial pressures. Mortgage repayments are expected to rise by an average of £80 per month, while food price inflation could reach 4.6% by autumn. Utility bills are also set to increase in July and remain elevated throughout the winter.

Inflation is now forecast to peak above 3.5% by the end of the year, a sharp increase from pre-conflict estimates. In a worst-case scenario, where oil prices climb to $130 a barrel and remain high for an extended period, inflation could exceed 6%. Such conditions might necessitate interest rate hikes of more than 1.5 percentage points, potentially pushing the base rate to at least 5.25%.

Why Rates Were Kept on Hold

Despite these inflationary pressures, the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) chose to maintain the current interest rate. Only one member, Huw Pill, the Bank’s chief economist, dissented, advocating for a rate increase. The decision reflects concerns about the UK’s fragile economic recovery, which could be further weakened by aggressive monetary tightening.

The MPC’s primary focus is on mitigating "second-round effects," where businesses pass on higher costs to consumers through price increases, and workers demand higher wages to offset rising living expenses. These dynamics could embed inflation more deeply into the economy. However, with GDP growth expected to be just 0.8% this year and unemployment projected to peak at 5.5% in 2024, the ability of workers to secure pay rises remains limited.

What This Means for Households

For UK households, the Bank’s decision offers short-term relief but underscores the challenges ahead. Rising energy costs, higher mortgage payments, and increased food prices are likely to squeeze household budgets. Meanwhile, cautious consumer spending could further dampen economic growth, creating a complex balancing act for policymakers.

Borrowers might consider reviewing their mortgage arrangements to prepare for potential rate increases in the future. Fixed-rate deals could provide stability amidst uncertainty, while homeowners may wish to explore options for reducing monthly outgoings where possible.

As the Bank of England navigates these turbulent economic waters, its cautious approach highlights the delicate interplay between inflation control and economic recovery. With global uncertainty showing no signs of abating, UK households and businesses must remain vigilant in managing their finances.

item.title: The Guardian view on help to buy: entrenching housing inequalities, rather than helping | Editorial
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Help to Buy scheme, introduced by the Conservative government, has come under scrutiny following a report by the Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS). The analysis reveals that the policy disproportionately benefited higher earners, raising questions about its effectiveness in addressing housing inequalities. Rather than aiding first-time buyers or those on lower incomes, the scheme appears to have accelerated wealth accumulation for those already in a position to purchase property.

Who Benefited Most from Help to Buy?

According to the IFS, the top 10% of earners reaped the largest financial gains from Help to Buy. The scheme enabled many to buy properties earlier or invest in more expensive homes, effectively allowing them to build wealth at a faster rate. Whilst the policy aimed to make homeownership more accessible, its impact on the housing market has been controversial. Critics argue that it pushed property prices higher in certain areas and increased competition without significantly boosting housing supply.

This outcome has highlighted concerns about the scheme’s role in widening housing and wealth inequalities. During its implementation, local authorities faced substantial cuts to housing budgets, with net spending per person reduced by 35% over a 12-year period. Similarly, spending on planning and development fell by a third, raising questions about the government’s priorities in addressing the housing crisis.

A Missed Opportunity for Housing Supply?

The Help to Buy scheme has also been criticised for failing to tackle the structural issues driving up housing costs. A 2022 report by the House of Lords built environment committee noted that the policy did not effectively increase housing supply or provide good value for money. Instead of extending the scheme, experts suggested that funding local authorities and housing associations to build homes could have been a more impactful approach.

By focusing on private ownership, Help to Buy diverted resources that might have been better spent on social housing or local authority planning budgets. Investing in social housing could have not only supported lower-income households but also stabilised the construction industry by smoothing out market fluctuations. Studies from countries like Austria have shown that such investments can help maintain construction activity even during periods of buyer hesitancy.

Whilst Help to Buy aimed to support aspiring homeowners, its outcomes have sparked debate about its broader impact on housing inequality and market dynamics. As discussions continue about future housing policies, there is growing recognition of the need for strategies that address supply shortages and support a wider range of households. Homeowners and policymakers alike may wish to consider alternative approaches that balance affordability, supply, and long-term sustainability in the housing market.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Help to Buy scheme, introduced by the Conservative government, has come under scrutiny following a report by the Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS). The analysis reveals that the policy disproportionately benefited higher earners, raising questions about its effectiveness in addressing housing inequalities. Rather than aiding first-time buyers or those on lower incomes, the scheme appears to have accelerated wealth accumulation for those already in a position to purchase property.

Who Benefited Most from Help to Buy?

According to the IFS, the top 10% of earners reaped the largest financial gains from Help to Buy. The scheme enabled many to buy properties earlier or invest in more expensive homes, effectively allowing them to build wealth at a faster rate. Whilst the policy aimed to make homeownership more accessible, its impact on the housing market has been controversial. Critics argue that it pushed property prices higher in certain areas and increased competition without significantly boosting housing supply.

This outcome has highlighted concerns about the scheme’s role in widening housing and wealth inequalities. During its implementation, local authorities faced substantial cuts to housing budgets, with net spending per person reduced by 35% over a 12-year period. Similarly, spending on planning and development fell by a third, raising questions about the government’s priorities in addressing the housing crisis.

A Missed Opportunity for Housing Supply?

The Help to Buy scheme has also been criticised for failing to tackle the structural issues driving up housing costs. A 2022 report by the House of Lords built environment committee noted that the policy did not effectively increase housing supply or provide good value for money. Instead of extending the scheme, experts suggested that funding local authorities and housing associations to build homes could have been a more impactful approach.

By focusing on private ownership, Help to Buy diverted resources that might have been better spent on social housing or local authority planning budgets. Investing in social housing could have not only supported lower-income households but also stabilised the construction industry by smoothing out market fluctuations. Studies from countries like Austria have shown that such investments can help maintain construction activity even during periods of buyer hesitancy.

Whilst Help to Buy aimed to support aspiring homeowners, its outcomes have sparked debate about its broader impact on housing inequality and market dynamics. As discussions continue about future housing policies, there is growing recognition of the need for strategies that address supply shortages and support a wider range of households. Homeowners and policymakers alike may wish to consider alternative approaches that balance affordability, supply, and long-term sustainability in the housing market.

item.title: Bank bosses called to meeting with Reeves over impact of Iran war on UK economy
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK government has taken steps to address the potential economic fallout from the escalating conflict in the Middle East, with Chancellor Rachel Reeves convening an emergency meeting with the heads of Britain’s largest retail banks. Scheduled for this week, the summit will bring together executives from HSBC, Barclays, Lloyds, NatWest, and Santander to discuss strategies for mitigating the impact of the crisis on the UK economy.

Focus on Mortgage Borrowers

One of the key areas of discussion will be the protection of mortgage borrowers, particularly those facing the end of their fixed-rate deals. With over 1.6 million homeowners expected to see their current mortgage terms expire by the end of the year, the meeting will review banks’ commitments under the government’s mortgage charter. Lenders have already begun contacting affected customers to outline their options, but further measures may be needed to shield households from rising costs.

The conflict, which has seen Iran retaliate against US and Israeli strikes by closing the Strait of Hormuz and targeting neighbouring oil producers, has driven energy prices higher. This, in turn, has led to concerns about inflationary pressures and potential increases in mortgage rates. The Bank of England has warned that more than one million households could face higher loan servicing costs, highlighting the importance of lender forbearance in preventing a significant economic shock.

Impact on the Mortgage Market

The Middle East crisis has already had a noticeable effect on the UK mortgage market. Earlier this month, the Bank of England’s Financial Policy Committee reported that lenders had withdrawn around 1,500 mortgage products and raised interest rates on many of the remaining 7,000. This has created additional pressure for homeowners preparing to secure new mortgage agreements.

Looking ahead, the Bank of England estimates that approximately 5.2 million borrowers—around 58% of the total mortgage market—could see their payments rise by the end of 2028. Against this backdrop, the government is urging banks to provide early insights into consumer behaviour in response to the crisis, which could inform future policy decisions.

As banks prepare to release their latest financial results, the outcomes of this meeting could play a crucial role in shaping their approach to supporting customers and stabilising the economy during a period of heightened uncertainty.

The summit underscores the government’s proactive stance in addressing the potential economic repercussions of global conflicts. With mortgage borrowers and the broader economy in focus, the discussions could pave the way for measures designed to protect vulnerable households and maintain financial stability.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK government has taken steps to address the potential economic fallout from the escalating conflict in the Middle East, with Chancellor Rachel Reeves convening an emergency meeting with the heads of Britain’s largest retail banks. Scheduled for this week, the summit will bring together executives from HSBC, Barclays, Lloyds, NatWest, and Santander to discuss strategies for mitigating the impact of the crisis on the UK economy.

Focus on Mortgage Borrowers

One of the key areas of discussion will be the protection of mortgage borrowers, particularly those facing the end of their fixed-rate deals. With over 1.6 million homeowners expected to see their current mortgage terms expire by the end of the year, the meeting will review banks’ commitments under the government’s mortgage charter. Lenders have already begun contacting affected customers to outline their options, but further measures may be needed to shield households from rising costs.

The conflict, which has seen Iran retaliate against US and Israeli strikes by closing the Strait of Hormuz and targeting neighbouring oil producers, has driven energy prices higher. This, in turn, has led to concerns about inflationary pressures and potential increases in mortgage rates. The Bank of England has warned that more than one million households could face higher loan servicing costs, highlighting the importance of lender forbearance in preventing a significant economic shock.

Impact on the Mortgage Market

The Middle East crisis has already had a noticeable effect on the UK mortgage market. Earlier this month, the Bank of England’s Financial Policy Committee reported that lenders had withdrawn around 1,500 mortgage products and raised interest rates on many of the remaining 7,000. This has created additional pressure for homeowners preparing to secure new mortgage agreements.

Looking ahead, the Bank of England estimates that approximately 5.2 million borrowers—around 58% of the total mortgage market—could see their payments rise by the end of 2028. Against this backdrop, the government is urging banks to provide early insights into consumer behaviour in response to the crisis, which could inform future policy decisions.

As banks prepare to release their latest financial results, the outcomes of this meeting could play a crucial role in shaping their approach to supporting customers and stabilising the economy during a period of heightened uncertainty.

The summit underscores the government’s proactive stance in addressing the potential economic repercussions of global conflicts. With mortgage borrowers and the broader economy in focus, the discussions could pave the way for measures designed to protect vulnerable households and maintain financial stability.

item.title: Higher-income households benefited most from Help to Buy, thinktank finds
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Help to Buy schemes, introduced in 2013 by the coalition government, aimed to make homeownership more accessible during a period of rising house prices. However, a recent analysis by the Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS) suggests that higher-income households were the primary beneficiaries, with limited impact on social mobility.

How Help to Buy Worked

Help to Buy comprised two main initiatives. The first offered taxpayer-backed loans to reduce the deposit required for purchasing a home, whilst the second provided a mortgage guarantee scheme, covering part of lenders’ potential losses on high loan-to-value mortgages. Both schemes applied to homes worth up to £600,000 and supported around one-fifth of first-time buyer purchases by 2014-15.

However, the IFS analysis indicates that these schemes predominantly aided higher-income households, particularly those outside London and the south-east of England, where property prices were comparatively lower. These individuals were likely to have been able to save for a deposit without the schemes, meaning Help to Buy may have accelerated their home purchase by a few years rather than making homeownership possible in the long term.

Limited Impact on Affordability and Social Mobility

The mortgage guarantee scheme had “limited effects on affordability”, according to the IFS. Borrowers remained constrained by income multiples, limiting the amount they could borrow. Meanwhile, the loan scheme, which applied exclusively to new-build properties, had a narrower scope and was described as “muted in effect”.

The report highlights that whilst Help to Buy may have helped some first-time buyers onto the property ladder, its broader impact on social mobility was minimal. Higher-income households, who would have eventually purchased a home regardless, saw the greatest benefits. This raises questions about the effectiveness of such schemes in addressing housing affordability for lower-income groups.

Bee Boileau, a research economist at the IFS and co-author of the briefing, noted: “Help to Buy policies can help first-time buyers get on the housing ladder in theory, but they can also push up house prices and require the government to assume risks on loans that the private sector might not otherwise take.”

For homeowners considering their options, it could be worth exploring alternative schemes or savings strategies to achieve their property goals.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Help to Buy schemes, introduced in 2013 by the coalition government, aimed to make homeownership more accessible during a period of rising house prices. However, a recent analysis by the Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS) suggests that higher-income households were the primary beneficiaries, with limited impact on social mobility.

How Help to Buy Worked

Help to Buy comprised two main initiatives. The first offered taxpayer-backed loans to reduce the deposit required for purchasing a home, whilst the second provided a mortgage guarantee scheme, covering part of lenders’ potential losses on high loan-to-value mortgages. Both schemes applied to homes worth up to £600,000 and supported around one-fifth of first-time buyer purchases by 2014-15.

However, the IFS analysis indicates that these schemes predominantly aided higher-income households, particularly those outside London and the south-east of England, where property prices were comparatively lower. These individuals were likely to have been able to save for a deposit without the schemes, meaning Help to Buy may have accelerated their home purchase by a few years rather than making homeownership possible in the long term.

Limited Impact on Affordability and Social Mobility

The mortgage guarantee scheme had “limited effects on affordability”, according to the IFS. Borrowers remained constrained by income multiples, limiting the amount they could borrow. Meanwhile, the loan scheme, which applied exclusively to new-build properties, had a narrower scope and was described as “muted in effect”.

The report highlights that whilst Help to Buy may have helped some first-time buyers onto the property ladder, its broader impact on social mobility was minimal. Higher-income households, who would have eventually purchased a home regardless, saw the greatest benefits. This raises questions about the effectiveness of such schemes in addressing housing affordability for lower-income groups.

Bee Boileau, a research economist at the IFS and co-author of the briefing, noted: “Help to Buy policies can help first-time buyers get on the housing ladder in theory, but they can also push up house prices and require the government to assume risks on loans that the private sector might not otherwise take.”

For homeowners considering their options, it could be worth exploring alternative schemes or savings strategies to achieve their property goals.

item.title: ‘We’re trapped’: despair for sellers as Iran war knocks confidence in UK housing market
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK Housing Market Feels the Ripple Effects of Global Uncertainty

The UK housing market is experiencing tremors as global instability, including recent Middle East tensions, contributes to wavering consumer confidence. Estate agents across the country report a noticeable shift in sentiment, with potential buyers and sellers becoming increasingly cautious amidst rising mortgage costs and economic uncertainty.

In historic cities like Canterbury, where the property market typically thrives during spring, activity has slowed considerably. Local agents observe that while homeowners continue requesting valuations, far fewer are proceeding to list their properties compared to previous years. This hesitation suggests many are adopting a 'wait-and-see' approach during these volatile times.

First-Time Buyers Face Particular Challenges

The current climate appears particularly challenging for first-time buyers, who often have smaller deposits and less experience navigating turbulent markets. Reports suggest this group is increasingly withdrawing from purchases, creating fractures at the lower end of property chains. This trend could have long-term implications for market fluidity, as first-time buyers traditionally play a crucial role in maintaining movement throughout the housing ladder.

Estate agents note that where properties do come to market, pricing strategies have become more aggressive to attract cautious buyers. Homes that might have sparked bidding wars earlier in the year are now being listed at reduced prices to generate interest. Simultaneously, surveyors appear to be taking a more conservative approach to valuations, adding another layer of complexity to transactions.

Speed Becomes Crucial in Property Transactions

Industry professionals emphasise the importance of swift transactions in the current climate. The longer a sale takes to complete, the greater the risk of buyers experiencing 'cold feet' or lenders withdrawing products. Many agents are focusing on streamlining processes to minimise delays and reduce the likelihood of collapsed chains.

While certain segments of the market continue to function - particularly where life circumstances necessitate a move - the overall picture suggests a period of adjustment for the UK housing market. Homeowners considering selling might benefit from realistic pricing expectations, while buyers could find opportunities in this more cautious environment.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK Housing Market Feels the Ripple Effects of Global Uncertainty

The UK housing market is experiencing tremors as global instability, including recent Middle East tensions, contributes to wavering consumer confidence. Estate agents across the country report a noticeable shift in sentiment, with potential buyers and sellers becoming increasingly cautious amidst rising mortgage costs and economic uncertainty.

In historic cities like Canterbury, where the property market typically thrives during spring, activity has slowed considerably. Local agents observe that while homeowners continue requesting valuations, far fewer are proceeding to list their properties compared to previous years. This hesitation suggests many are adopting a 'wait-and-see' approach during these volatile times.

First-Time Buyers Face Particular Challenges

The current climate appears particularly challenging for first-time buyers, who often have smaller deposits and less experience navigating turbulent markets. Reports suggest this group is increasingly withdrawing from purchases, creating fractures at the lower end of property chains. This trend could have long-term implications for market fluidity, as first-time buyers traditionally play a crucial role in maintaining movement throughout the housing ladder.

Estate agents note that where properties do come to market, pricing strategies have become more aggressive to attract cautious buyers. Homes that might have sparked bidding wars earlier in the year are now being listed at reduced prices to generate interest. Simultaneously, surveyors appear to be taking a more conservative approach to valuations, adding another layer of complexity to transactions.

Speed Becomes Crucial in Property Transactions

Industry professionals emphasise the importance of swift transactions in the current climate. The longer a sale takes to complete, the greater the risk of buyers experiencing 'cold feet' or lenders withdrawing products. Many agents are focusing on streamlining processes to minimise delays and reduce the likelihood of collapsed chains.

While certain segments of the market continue to function - particularly where life circumstances necessitate a move - the overall picture suggests a period of adjustment for the UK housing market. Homeowners considering selling might benefit from realistic pricing expectations, while buyers could find opportunities in this more cautious environment.
